s2.q: Which part of the fetal skull is compressible during labour, allowing moulding?
s2.explain: The vault is compressible during labour — its five flat bones are joined by fibrous sutures that allow sliding. The base is rigid and non-compressible because bones are fused.

s2.q: The base of the fetal skull extends from:
s2.explain: The base extends from the chin to the foramen magnum and is a rigid, non-compressible ring.

s2.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s2.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s2.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s2.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s3.q: How many bones form the vault of the fetal skull?
s3.explain: The vault is built from 5 flat bones: 2 frontal bones, 2 parietal bones, and 1 occipital bone.

s3.q: Which suture separates the two frontal bones?
s3.explain: The frontal suture separates the two frontal bones. The sagittal suture separates the two parietal bones.
The lambdoid suture separates the occipital bone from the parietal bones.

s3.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s3.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s4.q: The vertex of the fetal skull is bounded anteriorly by:
s4.explain: The vertex is bounded anteriorly by the anterior fontanelle and coronal suture, posteriorly by the posterior fontanelle and lambdoid suture, and laterally by lines through the parietal eminences.

s4.q: The brow presentation has which engagement diameter?
s4.explain: In brow presentation, the presenting diameter is the mento-vertical (13.5 cm), which exceeds the largest pelvic inlet diameter, so the head cannot enter the pelvis.

s4.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s4.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s4.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s5.q: How many fontanelles are there in the fetal skull, and how many are obstetrically important?
s5.explain: There are 6 fontanelles in the fetal skull. Only 2 are obstetrically important: the anterior and posterior fontanelles. The other 4 lie at the ends of the temporal sutures.

s5.q: The anterior and posterior fontanelles help diagnose all EXCEPT:
options: Degree of flexion of the head
s5.explain: The fontanelles help diagnose vertex presentation, the position of the occiput (by distinguishing diamond vs triangular shape), and the degree of flexion. They do not determine fetal sex.

s5.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s5.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s5.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

options: It is essential for understanding the condition

s5.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s6.q: The anterior fontanelle (bregma) is surrounded by how many bones?
s6.explain: The anterior fontanelle is surrounded by 4 bones: 2 frontal and 2 parietal. It is large, lozenge-shaped, and closes about 1.5 years after birth.

s6.q: Which fontanelle has a bony floor at term?
s6.explain: The posterior fontanelle has a bony floor and its surrounding bones overlap during moulding. The anterior fontanelle has a membranous floor.
The posterior fontanelle closes (is completely ossified) at full term. Memory aid: Posterior = Pre-birth/at term.

s6.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s8.q: Which diameter is the engagement diameter in occipito-anterior position with complete flexion?
s8.explain: The suboccipito-bregmatic diameter (9.5 cm) is the engagement diameter in occipito-anterior position with complete flexion — it is the smallest diameter presenting.

s8.q: The occipito-frontal diameter (11.5 cm) is the engagement diameter in:
s8.explain: The occipito-frontal diameter (11.5 cm) is the engagement diameter in occipito-posterior position and also distends the vulva in face-to-pubis delivery.

options: The mento-vertical diameter (13.5 cm) exceeds the pelvic inlet diameters
s8.explain: The mento-vertical diameter (13.5 cm) is longer than the largest pelvic inlet diameter (anatomical transverse = 13 cm), so the head cannot enter the pelvis in brow presentation.

s8.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition

It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s8.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s8.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s8.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s9.q: Which is the most important transverse diameter of the fetal skull?
s9.explain: The biparietal diameter (9.5 cm) is the most important transverse diameter — it is the standard sonographic measurement for estimating fetal head size and confirming engagement.

s9.q: The subparietal supraparietal diameter (9 cm) is the engagement diameter in:
s9.explain: The subparietal supraparietal diameter is the engagement diameter in asynclitism, where the head is tilted laterally in the pelvis.
